title: COCOBOD Staff Trained on RTI to Boost Information Access
excerpt: The officer in charge of the Right to Information (RTI) Desk at COCOBOD led an insightful training session for CHED staff on transparency and information accessibility.
author: CHED Communications
content: The officer in charge of the Right to Information (RTI) Desk at Ghana Cocoa Board (COCOBOD), led an insightful training session for staff of the Cocoa Health and Extension Division (CHED) to enhance transparency and information accessibility across the division.

The comprehensive training session covered the fundamentals of the Right to Information Act, its application within COCOBOD, and the responsibilities of public institutions in ensuring information accessibility to citizens.

Participants were taken through the procedures for handling RTI requests, the timeline for responses, and the exemptions under the law. The training emphasized the importance of proactive disclosure and the role of information officers in facilitating access to information.

In her opening remarks, the RTI Desk Officer highlighted that the training was part of COCOBOD's commitment to promoting transparency and accountability in its operations, in line with the government's transparency agenda.

Staff were encouraged to familiarize themselves with the RTI procedures and to support the implementation of the Act within their respective departments. The training session included practical exercises and case studies to help participants understand real-world applications of the RTI framework.
The session concluded with an interactive question and answer segment, where staff had the opportunity to seek clarification on various aspects of the RTI implementation within the cocoa sector.

title: Fire Safety Training Enhances Workplace Preparedness
excerpt: CHED staff participated in a comprehensive fire safety training exercise, learning essential fire prevention techniques and emergency response procedures.
author: CHED Communications
content: Staff of the Cocoa Health and Extension Division (CHED) have participated in a comprehensive fire safety training exercise as part of ongoing efforts to ensure workplace safety and emergency preparedness.
The training, facilitated by certified fire safety experts, covered essential fire prevention techniques, proper use of firefighting equipment, and emergency response procedures.

Participants received hands-on training in the use of fire extinguishers, fire blankets, and other firefighting equipment. They were also educated on the different classes of fires and the appropriate extinguishing methods for each type.

The facilitators emphasized the importance of fire prevention measures, including proper storage of flammable materials, regular inspection of electrical installations, and maintaining clear evacuation routes.
A practical demonstration session allowed staff to practice using fire extinguishers on controlled fires, building confidence in their ability to respond effectively in emergency situations.
Management reaffirmed its commitment to providing a safe working environment for all staff and announced that similar training exercises would be conducted regularly at all CHED regional offices.

title: Community Engagement Strengthens Cocoa Farmer Relations
excerpt: The division conducted extensive community outreach programs, engaging with cocoa farmers across multiple regions to share best practices.
author: CHED Communications
content: The Cocoa Health and Extension Division (CHED) has conducted extensive community outreach programs across multiple cocoa-growing regions, engaging directly with farmers to strengthen partnerships and share best practices.

The community engagement sessions brought together hundreds of cocoa farmers, community leaders, and extension officers to discuss challenges facing cocoa production and explore collaborative solutions.

During these interactive sessions, farmers had the opportunity to voice their concerns, ask questions, and receive expert advice on cocoa cultivation, disease management, and sustainable farming practices.
Extension officers provided demonstrations on proper pruning techniques, fermentation methods, and farm management practices aimed at improving yield and quality.
The Division used these engagements to introduce new initiatives, including improved seedling distribution programs and mobile extension services designed to reach remote farming communities.

Community leaders expressed appreciation for CHED's proactive approach to farmer engagement and committed to working closely with extension officers to ensure the success of cocoa development programs in their areas.

title: Cocoa Rehabilitation Program Shows Significant Progress
excerpt: Over 45,000 hectares of cocoa farms have been rehabilitated under the national cocoa rehabilitation program, benefiting thousands of farmers.
author: CHED Communications
content: The national cocoa rehabilitation program has achieved significant milestones, with over 45,000 hectares of diseased and aged cocoa farms rehabilitated across the country.
The program, implemented by CHED with support from COCOBOD, targets cocoa farms affected by the Cocoa Swollen Shoot Virus Disease (CSSVD) and other productivity-limiting factors.

Rehabilitated farms have been supplied with improved hybrid cocoa seedlings that are more resistant to diseases and have higher yield potential. Farmers have also received training on best practices for establishing and maintaining their new plantations.

Regional Coordinators report that the rehabilitation program has created employment opportunities in rural communities, with many youth engaged in land preparation, planting, and farm maintenance activities.

Beneficiary farmers have expressed satisfaction with the program, noting improvements in farm productivity and income prospects. Many have committed to adopting the recommended agronomic practices to ensure sustainable production.
CHED management has indicated plans to scale up the rehabilitation program in the coming year, with a target of rehabilitating an additional 20,000 hectares of cocoa farms.

title: Extension Officers Complete Advanced Training Program
excerpt: A new cohort of extension officers has completed intensive training on modern cocoa farming techniques and farmer engagement strategies.
author: CHED Communications
content: A new cohort of extension officers has completed an intensive advanced training program on modern cocoa farming techniques and farmer engagement strategies.

The three-week training program, organized by CHED in collaboration with the Cocoa Research Institute of Ghana (CRIG), equipped participants with up-to-date knowledge on cocoa agronomy, pest and disease management, and extension service delivery.
Participants were trained on the use of digital tools for data collection and farmer registration, enabling more efficient service delivery and monitoring of farm-level activities.
The program included field visits to model cocoa farms and research stations, providing practical exposure to innovative farming practices and technologies.

Facilitators from CRIG and other partner institutions delivered modules on climate-smart agriculture, integrated pest management, and sustainable cocoa production practices aligned with international standards.
The newly trained officers have been deployed to various regions to support ongoing extension programs and to enhance the quality of services delivered to cocoa farmers.

title: CHED Strengthens International Partnerships
excerpt: Senior management held productive discussions with international partners on collaborative programs to support Ghana's cocoa industry.
author: CHED Communications
content: Senior management of the Cocoa Health and Extension Division (CHED) has held productive discussions with representatives from international partner organizations on collaborative programs to support Ghana's cocoa industry.
The meeting, held at COCOBOD headquarters in Accra, reviewed ongoing partnership programs and explored new areas of collaboration in research, capacity building, and farmer support initiatives.

Discussions focused on sustainable cocoa production, traceability systems, and programs to improve farmer livelihoods. Partners expressed commitment to supporting Ghana's efforts to maintain its position as a leading producer of premium quality cocoa.

Key outcomes of the meeting included agreements on joint research initiatives, technical assistance programs, and capacity building for extension officers. Partners also committed to supporting climate adaptation programs for cocoa farmers.

CHED management presented the Division's strategic priorities for the next five years, highlighting areas where partnership support would be most impactful. Partners welcomed the strategic direction and indicated willingness to align their programs with national priorities.
The meeting concluded with a commitment to regular engagement and joint monitoring of partnership programs to ensure effective implementation and measurable impact on cocoa farming communities.
